records.ety: [OE. feste festival, holiday, feast, OF. feste festival, F. fte, fr. L. festum, pl. festa, fr. festus joyful, festal; of uncertain origin. Cf. Fair, n., Festal, Fte.]
usage: -- Feast, Banquet, Festival, Carousal. A feast sets before us viands superior in quantity, variety, and abundance; a banquet is a luxurious feast; a festival is the joyful celebration by good cheer of some agreeable event. Carousal is unrestrained indulgence in frolic and drink.

defs: A festival; a holiday; a solemn, or more commonly, a joyous, anniversary.
quotes: The seventh day shall be a feast to the Lord.
Now his parents went to Jerusalem every year at the feast of the passover.
notes: ☞ An Ecclesiastical feast is called a immovable feast when it always occurs on the same day of the year; otherwise it is called a movable feast. Easter is a notable movable feast.

defs: A festive or joyous meal; a grand, ceremonious, or sumptuous entertainment, of which many guests partake; a banquet characterized by tempting variety and abundance of food.
quotes: Enough is as good as a feast.
Belshazzar the King made a great feast to a thousand of his lords.

defs: That which is partaken of, or shared in, with delight; something highly agreeable; entertainment.
quotes: The feast of reason, and the flow of soul.

records.ety: [OE. festen, cf. OF. fester to rest from work, F. fter to celebrate a holiday. See Feast, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Feasted; p. pr. & vb. n. Feasting.]

defs: To eat sumptuously; to dine or sup on rich provisions, particularly in large companies, and on public festivals.
quotes: And his sons went and feasted in their houses.

defs: To be highly gratified or delighted.
quotes: With my love's picture then my eye doth feast.

defs: To entertain with sumptuous provisions; to treat at the table bountifully; as, he was feasted by the king.
To delight; to gratify; as, to feast the soul.
quotes: Feast your ears with the music a while.
